mappings.rationaleAI: Hinduism from the Indus Valley civilization (c. 2500 BC) through Vedic religion covering the character of traditional Indian civilization (A). Addresses developments in religion during the Indus civilization (B.2.c), social organization and religious development in early Ganges cultures (B.3.a), caste system emergence with Brahman, Ksatriya, Vaisya, and Sudra castes (B.3.b), and growth of Hindu sects in South Indian cultural development (B.7.b). Covers social, economic, and cultural developments including Tantrism (D.3) and Hindu traditions across North India, the Deccan, and South India (C, D).

Indian philosophical traditions spanning 2,500 years covering six classical orthodox schools plus heterodox traditions, reflecting the character and intellectual achievements of Indian civilization (A). Vedic textual tradition connecting to early religious and linguistic developments (B.2.c). Growth of Tantrism and broader cultural developments (D.3).

The historical Buddha in 5th-century BC India, covering the early development of Buddhism alongside Jainism in the pre-Mauryan period (B.4.a). Teachings set in the context of ancient Indian society and the Ganges Valley religious landscape (B.3).

Buddhism originating in South Asia covering the early development of Buddhism in pre-Mauryan states (B.4.a). Asoka's patronage spreading Buddhism as consolidation of the Mauryan empire (B.5.a). Growth of Buddhist sects in South Indian civilization (B.7.b). Buddhist institutions across North India, the Deccan, and South India (C). Conversion of Ceylon to Buddhism (E.1).
Alexander's conquest reaching Pakistan, covering the invasion by Alexander the Great (327 BC) and establishment of Greek settlements in the Indian subcontinent (B.4.b).

Central Eurasian trade routes covering the rise of Indo-Greek and Asian rulers and various kingdoms in the north with trade connections (B.6), and the development of extensive maritime trade with the West in South Indian civilizations (B.7.a).

Archaeology covering archaeological sources for Indian civilization (A), Late Stone Age hunters and Neolithic settlement in Baluchistan and the Indus Valley (B.1), and the Indus civilization including development of urban centres like Mohenjo-daro and Harappa (B.2, B.2.a).
Contemporary India providing context for understanding the lasting character and achievements of traditional Indian civilizations and their modern continuity (A).
Dynastic rule covering succession and governance relevant to the Mauryan empire (B.5), the Guptas of North India (C.1), and the tripartite struggle and Rajput kingdoms in North India (D.1).
Hellenistic world extending to the Indus covering the establishment of Greek settlements after Alexander's invasion (B.4.b) and Indo-Greek rulers in the rise of small kingdoms in the north (B.6).
Southeast Asia covering Indian civilization's influence on Ceylonese and Southeast Asian civilizations through mandala kingdoms and the transmission of Hindu and Buddhist cultural traditions (A).

Pakistan covering the Indus valley civilization spanning 4,000 years, directly relevant to the geography and ethnography of the Indian subcontinent (A), Neolithic settlement in Baluchistan and the Indus Valley (B.1), and the Indus civilization's urban development (B.2).

Indian cinema addressing caste discrimination, gender oppression, and religious intolerance provides modern cultural context for understanding the enduring character and social structures of Indian civilization (A).
